census_population.description: US Census Bureau annual county- and state-level resident population estimates from 2020 to 2023. Contains a national file covering all US counties plus a state-specific file for Missouri. Geographic areas are identified by plain-text name (e.g., '.Autauga County, Alabama') with no embedded FIPS code column; FIPS codes must be joined from a lookup table.
census_population.purpose: Provides population denominators for computing Salmonella incidence rates per capita at the county or state level. Essential for normalizing outbreak case counts from PulseNet or NORS against local population size.

files.notes: National file. Header is on row 4 (0-indexed row 3). Row 0 is the US total. County rows are prefixed with a leading dot. Columns 2-6 are numeric population estimates; columns 0-1 are the geographic name and 2020 Census base population. 6 rows contain nulls (likely state subtotals or footnote rows).

Missouri-only county-level population file. Header is malformed (first row contains the table description string). Must be read with header=None or skipped rows. Contains same column structure: geographic area name + 2020 census base + 4 annual estimates.

census_population.loading_instructions:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_excel('2020-2023.xlsx', sheet_name='CO-EST2023-POP', header=3)
df.columns = ['Geographic_Area', 'Census_2020_Base', 'Estimate_2020', 'Estimate_2021', 'Estimate_2022', 'Estimate_2023']
# Drop footnotes/summary rows
df = df[df['Geographic_Area'].notna() & ~df['Geographic_Area'].str.startswith(('NOTE','Source'), na=False)]
# US total is row 0 (Geographic_Area == 'United States')
# Counties start with '.', strip dot: df['Geographic_Area'] = df['Geographic_Area'].str.lstrip('.')

Geographic_Area.description: County or state name. Counties are prefixed with a leading dot (e.g., '.Autauga County, Alabama'). First row is 'United States'.

Census_2020_Base.description: April 1 2020 Census base population count used as the starting point for estimates.

census_population.time_coverage: 2020-2023 (annual July 1 estimates, plus April 2020 Census base)
census_population.geographic_granularity: US county (plus one Missouri-only state file)
notable_quirks: No FIPS code column — geographic name is the only identifier. Must join to a FIPS lookup table using the county+state string.
County names have a leading dot prefix that must be stripped.
6 rows have null numeric values (likely state-level summary rows or footer notes that should be dropped).
The states/ subdirectory contains only Missouri; do not expect data for all states.
The national file (2020-2023.xlsx) already contains all county rows including Missouri — the state file is supplemental.

naco.description: The NACO (National Association of Counties) dataset contains only a links.txt file pointing to two external URLs: the County Economies 2024 report page and an interactive County Economies data explorer. No downloadable data files are present in the snapshot.
naco.purpose: County-level economic data (employment, wages, GDP) that could contextualize Salmonella outbreak burden relative to economic conditions. However, actual data files are NOT available in this snapshot — only web links are stored.

files.notes: Contains two URLs only: https://www.naco.org/resource/county-economies-2024-under-hood-national-economic-trends and https://ce.naco.org/?dset=County%20Economies&ind=County%20Economies%20Profiles. No CSV, Excel, or any tabular data file is present.

naco.loading_instructions:
# No data file to load. The snapshot contains only a text file with two URLs.
# To access data, visit: https://ce.naco.org/?dset=County%20Economies&ind=County%20Economies%20Profiles
# with open('links.txt') as f:
#     urls = f.read().strip().split('\n')
naco.time_coverage: 2024 (reference year for the linked report; actual data year coverage unknown without accessing the URLs)
notable_quirks: CRITICAL: This dataset snapshot contains NO tabular data. It is only a pointer (links.txt) to external web resources.
Agents should not attempt to load data from this path. Accessing county economic data requires fetching from the external URL.
The linked County Economies explorer may provide indicators like unemployment rate, per-capita income, and industry composition at county level.

nors.description: A Salmonella-focused subset of the CDC National Outbreak Reporting System (NORS). Contains 3,950 foodborne, animal-contact, and indeterminate/other outbreak records from 1998-2021. The main file (TROU_20230317_For Release2.xlsx) has 38 relational sheets linked by CDCID. An older companion file in NORS Produced data/ has the same structure plus a pivot-table summary sheet.
nors.purpose: Primary dataset for Salmonella outbreak analysis. Links outbreak events to exposure state/county, serotype (via GenEtiology sheet), implicated foods, case counts, hospitalization, deaths, age/sex distribution, and incubation/duration data.

files.notes: Primary multi-sheet workbook. NORSMain sheet is the fact table (3,950 rows x 82 columns). All other sheets are dimension/detail tables keyed by CDCID. Load NORSMain first, then join specific sheets as needed.
Data dictionary with column-level descriptions for every field across all sheets.
Earlier release of the same data with an additional NORS_Data sheet containing a pivot table (year x month counts). NORSMain and all dimension sheets are identical in structure to the main file.
An IFS() formula (not a CSV) mapping US state names to 2-digit FIPS state codes. Useful for deriving FIPS from ExposureState column.
Outlook .msg file with CDC data release instructions. Not machine-readable without special libraries.

nors.loading_instructions:
import pandas as pd
# Load main outbreak records
df_main = pd.read_excel('TROU_20230317_For Release2.xlsx', sheet_name='NORSMain')
# Load etiology (pathogen) details
df_etio = pd.read_excel('TROU_20230317_For Release2.xlsx', sheet_name='GenEtiology')
# Filter to Salmonella only (most but not all outbreaks)
df_salm = df_etio[df_etio['GenusName'] == 'Salmonella']
# Join: df_main.merge(df_salm, on='CDCID', how='left')
# Load implicated foods
df_food = pd.read_excel('TROU_20230317_For Release2.xlsx', sheet_name='FB_FoodImplicated')
# Load geographic expansion tables
df_states = pd.read_excel('TROU_20230317_For Release2.xlsx', sheet_name='States')  # multi-state rows
df_counties = pd.read_excel('TROU_20230317_For Release2.xlsx', sheet_name='Counties')  # multi-county rows

CDCID.description: Unique outbreak identifier. Primary key for all joins across sheets.

DateFirstIll.description: Date of illness onset for the first case in the outbreak.

yearfirstill.description: Year of first illness onset. Range 1998-2021.

ExposureState.description: Full US state name of primary exposure location.

ExposureCounty.description: County name of primary exposure. '***' means suppressed for privacy. 'Multicounty' means multiple counties involved.

MultiStateExposure.description: Flag (0/1): whether the outbreak spanned multiple states.

DeathsNum.description: Number of deaths associated with outbreak. Predominantly 0.

EstimatedPrimary.description: Estimated total primary cases including lab-confirmed.

GenusName.description: Pathogen genus. Dataset is predominantly Salmonella (4,158 of 4,249 rows).

SerotypeName.description: Salmonella serotype. Top serotypes: Enteritidis (1007), Typhimurium (517), Newport (300), Heidelberg (195), I 4,[5],12:i:- (154), Javiana (134).

CDCID.description: Foreign key to NORSMain. One row per state per outbreak (11,000 total rows).

State.description: Full state name for multi-state outbreak expansion.

ExposureStateFlag.description: 1 if this state is an exposure state for the outbreak.

ResidenceStateFlag.description: 1 if this state is a residence state for the outbreak.

CDCID.description: Foreign key to NORSMain. 6,406 total rows.

County.description: County name (no state, no FIPS) for multi-county outbreak expansion.

CDCID.description: Foreign key to NORSMain. 2,157 total rows.

FoodConfirmed.description: Whether the food implication is confirmed.

Imported.description: Whether the food item was imported.

nors.geographic_granularity: US state and county (state-level consistently populated; county-level partially suppressed or 'Multicounty')
notable_quirks: CDCID is the join key across all 38 sheets — every dimension sheet links back via CDCID.
All 3,950 outbreaks in NORSMain are primarily Salmonella (a small number of 64 outbreaks have secondary co-pathogens like Campylobacter in GenEtiology).
ExposureCounty has '***' for suppressed counties and 'Multicounty' for multi-county outbreaks — not a clean FIPS join target.
States sheet has 11,000 rows (one per state per outbreak), not 3,950 — outbreaks spanning multiple states have multiple rows here.
Heavy missingness in incubation/duration fields, age breakdown columns, and NEARSID/OHHABSID reference columns.
The STATEFP_NORS.txt file contains an Excel IFS() formula (not a CSV) mapping state names to FIPS codes for joining.
The file path includes a space: 'NORS Produced data/' — quote this path carefully in Python.

pulsenet.description: CDC PulseNet Salmonella isolate-level surveillance data. Contains 582,022 individual bacterial isolate records from US laboratories collected between 1996 and 2023, split across six time-period Excel files. Each record represents one Salmonella isolate with its source type (human patient, food, animal, environmental), serotype, WGS-based antigenic formula, patient demographics, geographic origin, and outbreak cluster assignment.
pulsenet.purpose: Definitive molecular surveillance dataset for Salmonella. Enables serotype trend analysis, outbreak cluster identification, demographic distribution of cases, geographic mapping of isolates at county/state level, and source attribution (human vs food vs environmental).

files.notes: Has an extra empty 'Unnamed: 21' column vs earlier files. Drop it after loading.
One data row (not one row per field) with column-level descriptions for all 21 fields. The column headers ARE the field names.

pulsenet.loading_instructions:
import pandas as pd

base = '/home/zayan/.cache/huggingface/hub/datasets--zayanhugsAI--pulsenet/snapshots/93b6ba578b340211d67ea353e77ed917ad798107/'
file_sheet_pairs = [
    ('Export_1996-2000.xlsx', '1996-2000'),
    ('Export_2001-2005.xlsx', '2001-2005'),
    ('Export_2006-2010.xlsx', '2006-2010'),
    ('Export_2011-2015.xlsx', '2011-2015'),
    ('Export_2016-2019.xlsx', '2016-2019'),
    ('Export_2020-2023.xlsx', '2020-2023'),
]
dfs = [pd.read_excel(base + f, sheet_name=s) for f, s in file_sheet_pairs]
df = pd.concat(dfs, ignore_index=True)
df['IsolatDate'] = pd.to_datetime(df['IsolatDate'], errors='coerce')
# Normalize inconsistent casing
df['SourceType'] = df['SourceType'].str.upper().str.strip()
df['PatientSex'] = df['PatientSex'].str.upper().str.strip()
# Drop empty extra column from 2020-2023 file
df = df.drop(columns=[c for c in df.columns if c.startswith('Unnamed')], errors='ignore')

Key.description: Unique isolate identifier. Format: '{LabID}___{number}'. Primary key.

Serotype.description: Salmonella serotype by conventional typing. Top serotypes in 2011-2015: Enteritidis (28,335), Typhimurium (21,278), Newport (18,300), Javiana (9,593), I 4,[5],12:i:- (8,194).

Serotype_wgs.description: WGS-derived serotype call. Populated only when WGS was performed.

AntigenForm_wgs.description: Full antigenic formula derived from WGS (e.g., 'I 17:k:e,n,x').

IsolatDate.dtype: object (string in Excel, convert to datetime)
IsolatDate.description: Date the isolate was collected. Stored as string in Excel. Range: 1996-2023 (a few outliers dated as early as 1970 are data entry errors).

LabID.description: 2-4 letter state lab code identifying the reporting laboratory (e.g., 'AK', 'TX', 'CA').

Outbreak.description: CDC outbreak cluster code. Null for ~87.5% of rows (sporadic cases). Non-null links to a specific outbreak investigation; can be matched to NORS CDCID records.

PatientAgeDays.description: Patient age in days (only for patients < 1 month old). ~99% null.

PatientAgeMonths.description: Patient age in months (only for patients < 1 year old). ~93% null.

PatientAgeYears.description: Patient age in whole years. ~7.6% null. Stored as object in some file vintages.

PatientSex.description: Patient sex: MALE, FEMALE, UNKNOWN. Inconsistent casing across file vintages — always normalize with .str.upper().str.strip().

PulseNet_UploadDate.description: Date the isolate was uploaded to the PulseNet national database.

SourceCity.description: City of patient residence or isolate collection (for non-human isolates). ~10% null.

SourceCounty.description: County name (not FIPS) of patient residence or isolate collection. ~0.005% null.

SourceState.description: 2-letter state abbreviation of patient/isolate origin. ~0.0003% null. Primary join key to other state-level datasets.

SourceCountry.description: Country where isolate was collected. ~99% are USA (with minor casing variants like 'USa'). Normalize before analysis.

SourceType.description: Source category with inconsistent casing across vintages. Always normalize to upper. Values: HUMAN (~98%), FOOD, ANIMAL, ENVIRONMENTAL, UNKNOWN.

SourceSite.description: Body site or specimen type (e.g., Blood, Stool, CSF) for human isolates. ~0.1% null.

TypeDetails.description: Further description of source (e.g., 'Bovine', 'Poultry', lot numbers for food items). ~97% null for human isolates.

WGS_id.description: De-identified WGS submission ID for NCBI. PNUSA prefix = PulseNet assigned. Only populated for WGS-performed isolates.

pulsenet.time_coverage: 1996-2023 (IsolatDate; a small number of records have dates as early as 1970 indicating data entry errors)
pulsenet.geographic_granularity: US county and state (SourceCounty, SourceState); also city-level (SourceCity)
notable_quirks: SourceType has inconsistent casing across file vintages ('Human', 'HUMAN', 'human'). Always normalize with .str.upper().str.strip() before grouping.
PatientSex similarly has mixed casing. Normalize before analysis.
Export_2020-2023.xlsx has 22 columns (an extra empty 'Unnamed: 21') vs 21 in all other files. Drop unnamed columns after concat.
Serotype is null for ~15.6% of rows (91,075 of 582,022). Many are labeled 'Serotype pending' in earlier files.
IsolatDate is stored as a string/object in Excel. Always convert with pd.to_datetime(..., errors='coerce').
SourceCounty contains the full county name (e.g., 'Autauga'), not a FIPS code. Joining to FIPS requires a county+state name lookup table.
The Outbreak column is null for ~87.5% of isolates (sporadic cases). Non-null values are CDC cluster codes linkable to NORS CDCIDs.
Age is split across three columns: PatientAgeDays (<1 month), PatientAgeMonths (<1 year), PatientAgeYears. Merge into a single age-in-years float for analysis.
A small fraction of SourceType values are state abbreviations (e.g., 'MO', 'AR') rather than source categories — these are data entry errors.

map_the_meal_gap.description: Feeding America's Map the Meal Gap (MMG) annual county- and state-level food insecurity estimates for the United States. Covers data years 2009 through 2020. Each annual file is a separate Excel workbook with County, State, and Congressional District sheets. FIPS codes are present in all files. Column naming conventions changed significantly across years.
map_the_meal_gap.purpose: Food insecurity rates at the county level serve as a social determinant of health proxy for Salmonella research. Enables correlation of Salmonella outbreak incidence or severity with food access/security disparities, economic hardship, and cost-of-food barriers to safe nutrition.

files.notes: Row 0 contains a methodological change warning. Use header=1 when loading this file.
Contains BOTH 2019 and 2020 data in a single sheet (6,285 rows = 2x counties). Has a 'Year' column. Adds race/ethnicity-specific FI rates and 'Weighted Annual Food Budget Shortfall'.

map_the_meal_gap.loading_instructions:
import pandas as pd

base = '/home/zayan/.cache/huggingface/hub/datasets--zayanhugsAI--map_the_meal_gap/snapshots/00ec583848152c3e1fb01dd2809c1850c577881a/'

# File-to-sheet mapping: (data_year, filename, sheet_name, header_row)
file_map = [
    (2009, 'MMG2011_2009Data_ToShare.xlsx', 'County', 0),
    (2010, 'MMG2012_2010Data_ToShare.xlsx', 'County', 0),
    (2011, 'MMG2013_2011Data_ToShare.xlsx', '2011 County', 0),
    (2012, 'MMG2014_2012Data_ToShare.xlsx', '2012 County', 0),
    (2013, 'MMG2015_2013Data_ToShare.xlsx', '2013 County', 0),
    (2014, 'MMG2016_2014Data_ToShare.xlsx', '2014 County', 0),
    (2015, 'MMG2017_2015Data_ToShare.xlsx', '2015 County', 0),
    (2016, 'MMG2018_2016Data_ToShare.xlsx', '2016 County', 0),
    (2017, 'MMG2019_2017Data_ToShare.xlsx', '2017 County', 0),
    (2018, 'MMG2020_2018Data_ToShare.xlsx', '2018 County', 1),
    (2019, 'MMG2021_2019Data_ToShare.xlsx', '2019 County', 0),
    ('2019-2020', 'MMG2022_2020-2019Data_ToShare.xlsx', 'County', 0),
]
dfs = []
for year, fname, sheet, hdr in file_map:
    df = pd.read_excel(base + fname, sheet_name=sheet, header=hdr)
    df = df[df['FIPS'].notna()]  # drop null/footnote rows
    df['data_year'] = year
    dfs.append(df)
# NOTE: column names differ across years. Identify FI rate column per-file before concat.
# Older files: 'FI Rate'; newer: '{year} Food Insecurity Rate'; 2022 file: 'Overall Food Insecurity Rate (1 Year)'

FIPS.description: 5-digit county FIPS code stored as a number. In older files stored as float (e.g., 2013.0). Zero-pad with str(int(x)).zfill(5) for string joins.

State.description: 2-letter state abbreviation. Column named 'State Name' in 2009 file, 'State' in later files.

County_State.description: County+state label. 2009 file: 'County Code' column with county name in caps. 2010+ files: 'County, State' column with 'Autauga County, Alabama' format.

FI_Rate.description: Food insecurity rate (proportion of county population that is food insecure). Column name varies by year: 'FI Rate' (2009), '{year} Food Insecurity Rate' (2010-2019), 'Overall Food Insecurity Rate (1 Year)' (2022 file).

Number_Food_Insecure.description: Estimated count of food-insecure persons in the county. Column name varies by year.

Child_FI_Rate.description: Food insecurity rate specifically among children.

Cost_Per_Meal.description: Estimated cost per meal in USD at county level.

Weighted_Annual_Dollars.description: Weighted annual food budget shortfall needed to address food insecurity. Named 'Weighted Annual Food Budget Shortfall' in 2022 file.

map_the_meal_gap.time_coverage: 2009-2020 (one file per data year; 2022 file covers both 2019 and 2020)
map_the_meal_gap.geographic_granularity: US county (primary); also US state and Congressional district sheets in each file
notable_quirks: CRITICAL: Column names change every single year. The food insecurity rate column is named differently in every file — always identify it programmatically or by file.
Sheet names also change: 'County' (2009, 2010, 2022 file), '{year} County' for 2011-2019.
MMG2020_2018Data_ToShare.xlsx has an extra warning row at row 0 ('Data from MMG 2020 are NOT directly comparable...'). Use header=1 for this file only.
The 2022 file (MMG2022_2020-2019Data_ToShare.xlsx) contains both 2019 and 2020 data in one sheet, yielding 6,285 rows. It adds a 'Year' column and race/ethnicity-specific FI rates.
FIPS is float in some files (e.g., 2013.0). Convert with int() before zero-padding to 5-digit string.
The 2009 file has 3,205 rows vs ~3,142-3,143 for other years due to boundary changes.
Approximately 64-96 rows per file are null (FIPS=NaN) — these are aggregate/footnote rows at the sheet bottom and must be dropped before analysis.
Note per the 2018 file warning: MMG 2020 data (covering 2018 estimates) uses revised methodology incompatible with prior years.

social_vulnerability_index.description: CDC/ATSDR Social Vulnerability Index (SVI) at US county level for years 2020 and 2022. Each file contains 158 columns covering 4 vulnerability themes: Socioeconomic Status (Theme 1), Household Characteristics (Theme 2), Racial & Ethnic Minority Status (Theme 3), Housing Type & Transportation (Theme 4), plus overall rankings. Variables include raw estimates (E_), percentages (EP_), percentile rankings (EPL_/RPL_), and flag counts (F_) for each indicator.
social_vulnerability_index.purpose: Key contextual dataset for Salmonella epidemiological research. SVI scores identify high-vulnerability counties where Salmonella outbreaks may cause disproportionate harm. Enables correlation of disease burden with social determinants (poverty, uninsurance, crowding, minority status, limited English proficiency) and adjustment for confounders in spatial epidemiology models.

files.notes: 2020 SVI data for all US counties. 3,143 rows x 158 columns. Zero null values in any column.
2022 SVI data. Identical 158-column structure to 2020 file. 3,144 rows. Zero null values.

social_vulnerability_index.loading_instructions:
import pandas as pd

base = '/home/zayan/.cache/huggingface/hub/datasets--zayanhugsAI--social_vulnerability_index/snapshots/9bb6f44cb04883ba56fd228c4ca50b922d0a4e1f/'
df_2020 = pd.read_csv(base + 'SVI_2020_US_county.csv')
df_2022 = pd.read_csv(base + 'SVI_2022_US_county.csv')
# FIPS is stored as integer — zero-pad for 5-character string joins:
df_2020['FIPS_str'] = df_2020['FIPS'].astype(str).str.zfill(5)
df_2022['FIPS_str'] = df_2022['FIPS'].astype(str).str.zfill(5)
# Key vulnerability scores:
# RPL_THEMES = overall SVI (0=least vulnerable, 1=most vulnerable)
# RPL_THEME1 = socioeconomic status
# RPL_THEME2 = household characteristics
# RPL_THEME3 = racial & ethnic minority status
# RPL_THEME4 = housing type & transportation

STCNTY.description: 5-digit FIPS state+county code as integer. Identical to FIPS column.

COUNTY.description: County name. In 2022 file uses short form (e.g., 'Autauga'); 2020 LOCATION column uses full form ('Autauga County, Alabama').

FIPS.description: 5-digit county FIPS code stored as integer. Alabama counties start at 1001 (not '01001'). Always zero-pad when converting to string for joins.

LOCATION.description: Full county+state string (e.g., 'Autauga County, Alabama'). Useful for display but use FIPS for joins.

AREA_SQMI.description: County land area in square miles.

EP_POV150.description: Percentage of persons below 150% of the federal poverty level.

EP_HBURD.description: Percentage of housing-cost-burdened occupied housing units.

EP_NOHSDP.description: Percentage of persons age 25+ with no high school diploma.

EP_UNINSUR.description: Percentage of civilian noninstitutionalized population without health insurance.

EP_AGE65.description: Percentage of persons aged 65 or older.

EP_MINRTY.description: Percentage minority (all races/ethnicities except non-Hispanic white).

EP_LIMENG.description: Percentage of population age 5+ who speak English less than 'very well' (limited English proficiency).

RPL_THEME1.description: Socioeconomic status theme percentile rank. 0=least vulnerable, 1=most vulnerable.

RPL_THEME2.description: Household characteristics theme percentile rank (age, disability, single-parent, limited English).

RPL_THEME3.description: Racial & ethnic minority status theme percentile rank.

RPL_THEME4.description: Housing type & transportation theme percentile rank (multi-unit, mobile homes, crowding, no vehicle, group quarters).

RPL_THEMES.description: Overall SVI score: percentile rank across all 4 themes combined. 0=least vulnerable county in the US, 1=most vulnerable.

F_TOTAL.description: Total count of vulnerability flag indicators triggered (out of 16 possible). A flag is set when a county is in the top 10% nationally for a given indicator.

notable_quirks: FIPS is stored as an integer (e.g., 1001 instead of '01001'). Always convert to zero-padded 5-digit string for joining with other datasets: df['FIPS'].astype(str).str.zfill(5).
Zero null values in either file — the SVI dataset is complete for all US counties in both years.

158 columns with systematic naming: E_ = raw count estimate, M_ = margin of error, EP_ = percentage, EPL_ = percentile rank of percentage, SPL_ = sum of theme percentile ranks, RPL_ = theme-level rank (0-1), F_ = binary flag (top 10% most vulnerable = 1).
The COUNTY column in 2022 omits the 'County' suffix (e.g., 'Autauga') while 2020's LOCATION column uses 'Autauga County, Alabama'. Use FIPS for year-to-year joins.
E_DAYPOP (daytime population) and race-specific EP_AFAM, EP_HISP, EP_ASIAN, etc. are additional columns beyond the standard SVI core — added in more recent CDC SVI releases.
RPL values of 0.0000 indicate the least vulnerable county nationally; 1.0000 indicates the most vulnerable. The dataset contains no -999 sentinel values for missing data.
